data.title: CRBP: CRB-913 achieved early, significant weight loss with minimal adverse events in phase I obesity study
data.description:
CRB-913 showed strong safety and tolerability in a phase I SAD-MAD study, with minimal GI and neuropsychiatric adverse events and early, significant weight loss in participants with obesity. The drug’s peripherally restricted mechanism led to weight loss not driven by GI side effects, supporting its differentiated profile and potential for combination therapy.

data.title: Fitch Downgrades Invatlan's Long-Term IDRs to 'CCC', Maintains Rating Watch Negative
data.description:
Fitch Ratings-Monterrey-11 December 2025:

Fitch Ratings has downgraded Inversiones Atlantida, S.A.'s (Invatlan) Long-Term Foreign and Local Currency Issuer Default Ratings (IDRs) to 'CCC' from 'B', its senior debt rating to 'CCC' with a Recovery Rating of 'RR4' from 'B'/'RR4', and its Short-Term Foreign and Local Currency IDRs to 'C' from 'B'. Fitch has maintained the ratings on Rating Watch Negative (RWN).

The downgrades resulted from the high liquidity and execution risks related to the upcoming USD300 million debt maturity on May 19, 2026. Although the entity has plans to address the bond repayment, Fitch notes that implementation of such measures faces challenges, including the short time remaining until the bond's maturity, an increasingly uncertain local operating environment due to political instability, and the significant size of the bond in relation to the company's current and future liquidity.

The RWN on all ratings reflects the ratings' downside potential if Fitch sees additional delays in the strategy, increasing pressures and less room to meet the maturity debt. Fitch will continue monitoring the execution of Invatlan's liquidity strategy and expects to review the ratings again at least 90 days prior to the bond's maturity date. Future rating actions will be driven by the factual achievements (or lack thereof) in addressing this debt repayment.

Fitch has also downgraded to 'CCC(EXP)'/'RR4'/RWN and simultaneously withdrawn Invatlan's senior debt expected rating, as it is no longer expected to convert to a final rating.

Key Rating Drivers

Invatlan's Liquidity and Execution Risks Persist: Fitch considers Invatlan's liquidity management and contingent plans have become significantly less prudent over the past year, constraining flexibility ahead of the May 2026 bond maturity. Liquidity risk is elevated given the proximity of the maturity, and delays or shortfalls in the execution of identified available sources could further compress the company's capacity to meet obligations in a timely manner. Fitch believes that a timelier execution and materialization of Invatlan's strategy remains critical to mitigate near-term risks and preserve financial flexibility.

Main Subsidiary's Credit Profile Is Factored into Invatlan's Ratings, but Intrinsic Considerations at the Holding Level Are Increasingly Driving the Ratings: Invatlan's ratings are linked to the credit profile of its main subsidiary, Banco Atlantida, S.A. (Atlantida). The holding company is now rated various notches below Atlantida, primarily due to the heightened and rapidly deteriorating liquidity and refinancing risks, although the high double leverage is also considered. Fitch does not rule out the possibility of additional downgrades in the near future, which could lead to a greater rating differentiation between both entities due to the increased liquidity risks, even if double leverage does not change significantly.

Rating Sensitivities Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--Fitch expects to review the ratings and the RWN after assessing the materialization of Invatlan's contingency plan and their impact on its credit profile. If liquidity and execution risks increase significantly, Fitch does not rule out additional downgrades of the ratings to reflect the potentially increasing possibility of a default;

--A significant reduction in dividend transfers from Invatlan's main subsidiaries that ultimately affects its liquidity to service debt, or a sustained increase of double leverage to above 200%.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--Although it is not the immediate scenario given Invatlan's current challenges, the RWN could be resolved and the ratings eventually upgraded if Invatlan's near-term funding strategies are completed successfully to an extent that materially reduces the current and future liquidity, refinancing and execution risks. A certain degree of notching down from Atlantida's ratings, however, is expected to remain.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: KEY RATING DRIVERS

Invatlan's Senior Secured Notes: The rating on the senior secured notes is aligned with Invatlan's issuer ratings. Although the notes are secured, Fitch believes the collateral mechanism does not materially change default risk and/or enhance recovery prospects. Under Fitch's criteria, recoveries are considered average, consistent with a Recovery Rating of 'RR4'.

OTHER DEBT AND ISSUER RATINGS: RATING SENSITIVITIES

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Negative Rating Action/Downgrade

--The global senior secured debt rating would mirror any change to Invatlan's IDRs;

--Negative rating factors are not applicable for the expected rating as it has been withdrawn.

Factors that Could, Individually or Collectively, Lead to Positive Rating Action/Upgrade

--The global senior secured debt rating would mirror any change to Invatlan's IDRs;

--Positive rating factors are not applicable for the expected rating as it has been withdrawn.

Sources of Information

The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria.

REFERENCES FOR SUBSTANTIALLY MATERIAL SOURCE CITED AS KEY DRIVER OF RATING The principal sources of information used in the analysis are described in the Applicable Criteria. Public Ratings with Credit Linkage to other ratings

Invatlan's IDRs are linked to Banco Atlantida's IDRs.

ESG Considerations

Invatlan has an ESG Relevance Score of '4' for Management Strategy due to risks associated with the company's ability to execute its strategy to pay its senior unsecured debt. These execution risks have a negative impact on the credit profile and are relevant to the ratings in conjunction with other factors.

Invatlan has an ESG Relevance Score of '4' for Financial Transparency due to lagging or missing information disclosure. This has a negative impact on the credit profile and is relevant to the ratings in conjunction with other factors.

The highest level of ESG credit relevance is a score of '3', unless otherwise disclosed in this section. A score of '3' means ESG issues are credit-neutral or have only a minimal credit impact on the entity, either due to their nature or the way in which they are being managed by the entity. Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores are not inputs in the rating process; they are an observation on the relevance and materiality of ESG factors in the rating decision. For more information on Fitch's ESG Relevance Scores, visit www.fitchratings.com/topics/esg/products#esg-relevance-scores.

Inversiones Atlantida S.A.; Long Term Issuer Default Rating; Downgrade; CCC; Rating Watch Negative

; Short Term Issuer Default Rating; Downgrade; C; Rating Watch Negative

; Local Currency Long Term Issuer Default Rating; Downgrade; CCC; Rating Watch Negative

; Local Currency Short Term Issuer Default Rating; Downgrade; C; Rating Watch Negative

----senior secured; Long Term Rating; Downgrade; CCC; Rating Watch Negative

----senior secured; Long Term Rating; Downgrade; CCC(EXP); Rating Watch Negative

----senior secured; Long Term Rating; Withdrawn; WD

data.title: Peru central bank holds benchmark interest rate at 4.25%
data.description:
Peru's central bank on Thursday held its benchmark interest rate at 4.25% for the third straight meeting, in line with analysts' expectations.

The decision comes after Peru's statistics agency reported inflation up 0.11% in November, bringing the 12-month benchmark rate for the capital Lima and wider metropolitan area to 1.37% -towards the lower edge of the bank's 1% to 3% target range.

"Year-on-year inflation is projected to gradually approach the center of the target range in the coming months. Likewise, inflation excluding food and energy is estimated to remain around 2%," the central bank said in a statement.

Peru's central bank has gradually brought its rate down from a high of 7.75% it maintained through the first part of 2023.

The central bank added that an easing of global trade tensions would likely foster moderate economic growth.

The Andean nation remains one of South America's most economically stable countries despite regular political upheaval, including Congress' October ouster of President Dina Boluarte.

General elections are scheduled for April 2026.

data.title: Lululemon Expects New Strategy to Show Up in Inventory This Spring — Market Talk
data.description: Lululemon's current assortment doesn't reflect its current strategy, but that should change this spring, Finance Chief Meghan Frank says during a call with analysts. "The assortments you currently see in stores and online include certain styles that are not representative of our go forward vision for the brand," she says, noting the company has let product life cycles run too long in some of its key franchises. Lululemon has been working to bring a new energy into its assortment, and increase its speed and agility, she says. The company remains on track for new styles to make up 35% of its assortment in the spring, she says. (kelly.cloonan@wsj.com)

data.title: FIZZ: Gross margin improved despite lower sales volume, with strong liquidity and no outstanding debt
data.description:
Net sales and case volume declined slightly year-over-year, but higher average selling prices improved gross margin and kept net income stable. Cash position and working capital increased, with no debt outstanding and continued focus on health-oriented beverages.

data.title: NZX 50 Extends Gains but Points to Weekly Losses
data.description:
The NZX 50 rose 44 points, or 0.3%, to 13,440 in early deals on Friday, extending gains from the previous session, mainly supported by communication services, healthcare, consumer discretionary, and utilities.

Interest rate cuts by the Fed continued to lift sentiment, though it signaled fewer reductions for 2026.

Traders anticipated the release of a slew of economic data next week, including GDP figures for Q3, amid hopes of a better reading after the RBNZ cut interest rates.

On the data front, business PMI improved to the highest level in four months in November, driven by new orders, production, and employment, indicating an improvement in domestic demand.

Among early gainers were Fisher & Paykel (1.3%), Fonterra (1.1%), Mercury NZ (1.1%), Freightways (0.7%), Meridian Energy (0.9%), Infratil (0.7%), Ebos Group (0.6%), and ANZ Group (0.3%).

For the week, the index is heading for a 0.3% decline, after being flat in the previous week.

data.title: Cycle Group to Acquire Applied Therapeutics
data.description:
By Elias Schisgall

Cycle Group Holdings Limited will acquire by the pharmaceutical company Applied Therapeutics.

Cycle will buy Applied for an initial price of 8.8 cents a share, Applied said Thursday. The company had around 145.6 million shares outstanding as of Nov. 13, according to a filing with the Securities and Exchange Commission, implying an initial transaction value of roughly $12.8 million.

As part of the agreement Cycle will issue to shareholders non-transferrable contingent value rights entitling them to two, 10 cent payments per share. That money will be paid upon approval of Applied's treatments for galactosemia and Charcot-Marie-Tooth sorbitol dehydrogenase deficiency within eight years of closing. Investors will also get a 20 cent payment per share if sales from Applied's products exceed $200 million within 10 years of closing.

Applied is also issuing an unsecured promissory note to Cycle allowing it to take loans up to $8.5 million to use as working capital.

Write to Elias Schisgall at elias.schisgall@wsj.com
